claims.claim_text: Wastewater treatment plants face increasing pressure to achieve stringent nitrogen removal standards while maintaining cost-effective operations.

The discharge of nutrients into receiving waters represents one of the most serious environmental problems, prompting authorities to impose stricter standards for wastewater treatment plant operations.
The activated sludge process remains the dominant technology for biological nutrient removal in municipal wastewater treatment.

Different control algorithms have been developed ranging from simple constant aeration strategies to sophisticated model predictive control approaches, varying considerably in their information requirements and complexity.

Plant operating costs encompass multiple components including aeration energy, sludge disposal expenses, external carbon dosage requirements, and potential effluent fines for regulatory non-compliance.
The Benchmark Simulation Model No. 1 framework provides a standardized platform for evaluating different control strategies under consistent conditions.

The Benchmark Simulation Model represents a pre-denitrification plant configuration incorporating both anoxic and aerobic reactor compartments, utilizing the IWA Activated Sludge Model No. 1 to describe biological processes.
